sentence: Subjective:
The patient, Michelle, a 4-year-old mother of twins, presented with a complaint of knee pain after a skiing accident in Vermont.
She reports that her knee popped and she was unable to bear weight after falling while coming off the lift.
The pain is located on the outside of the knee and feels like it will buckle.
She is unable to bend the knee fully and experiences pain with straightening.
There is no numbness or tingling in her toes.
She denies any history of high blood pressure, diabetes, or other significant medical conditions.
She is currently taking ibuprofen for pain, which provides some relief.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications:
Ibuprofen 600-800mg every 8 hours as needed for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
The heart sounds are in a nice regular rate and rhythm, and there is no murmur on lung exam.
The lungs are clear to auscultation.

On the right knee examination, there is edema and an effusion, pain to palpation of the lateral knee, decreased flexion and extension, a positive Lachman sign, palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulse, and no leg edema in the ankle.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has a ruptured anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) in her right knee.
Problem list: Right knee pain; Right ACL tear; Knee effusion.
Plan:
I ordered a knee MRI to confirm the diagnosis.
I prescribed ibuprofen 600-800mg every 8 hours as needed for pain.
I recommended a knee brace to provide support.
I will follow up with the patient in a few days to discuss the MRI results and further management.
Follow-up: The patient will return in a few days to discuss the MRI results and further management.
